paper2.topic: Accounting Concepts and the Accounting Equation
paper2.context: Understanding the foundations of accounting — key concepts, the accounting equation and the classification of items — is essential before preparing any financial statements.

parts.question: State and explain FIVE accounting concepts, giving a brief example of each.

rubric.value: going concern: business assumed to continue operating e.g. assets valued at cost not liquidation value
accruals: income and expenses matched to period they relate to not when cash paid
prudence: anticipate losses immediately recognise profits only when realised
consistency: same accounting methods applied each period for comparability
business entity: business treated as separate from owner personal transactions excluded

parts.question: A business has the following: Cash $5,000 — Inventory $8,000 — Equipment $20,000 — Bank loan $12,000 — Trade payables $3,000. (i) Calculate total assets. (ii) Calculate total liabilities. (iii) Calculate owner's equity.

rubric.value: total liabilities = 12000 + 3000 = $15,000

parts.question: Distinguish between capital expenditure and revenue expenditure. Give TWO examples of each.

rubric.value: capital expenditure: spending on non-current assets providing benefit over multiple years e.g. buying machinery extending premises
revenue expenditure: day-to-day operating costs e.g. rent electricity wages repairs

paper2.strand: Introduction to Financial Statements and Ratios
paper2.unit: Introduction to Financial Statements and Ratios
paper2.topic: Income Statement and Statement of Financial Position
paper2.context: Xavier's Trading operates as a sole trader. The following information is available for the year ended 31 December 2024: Sales $95,000 — Sales returns $3,000 — Opening inventory $7,500 — Purchases $52,000 — Purchases returns $2,000 — Closing inventory $9,000 — Carriage inwards $800 — Wages $12,000 — Rent $4,500 — Electricity $1,200 — Carriage outwards $600 — Discount allowed $400.

parts.question: Prepare the Income Statement (Trading and Profit & Loss Account) for Xavier's Trading for the year ended 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: COGS = opening inventory 7500 + purchases 52000 − returns 2000 + carriage inwards 800 − closing inventory 9000 = $49,300
total expenses = wages 12000 + rent 4500 + electricity 1200 + carriage outwards 600 + discount allowed 400 = $18,700

parts.question: Calculate the gross profit margin and net profit margin for Xavier's Trading. Comment briefly on what each ratio shows.

rubric.value: gross profit margin = 42700/92000 × 100 = 46.4%
net profit margin = 24000/92000 × 100 = 26.1%
gross profit margin shows 46.4c of every dollar of sales becomes gross profit before overheads
net profit margin shows 26.1c of every dollar remains as net profit after all costs

paper2.strand: Introduction to Financial Statements and Ratios
paper2.unit: Introduction to Financial Statements and Ratios
paper2.topic: Statement of Financial Position and Ratios
paper2.context: The following balances appeared in the books of Maya's Enterprise at 31 December 2024: Premises $60,000 — Motor vehicle $18,000 — Inventory $9,500 — Trade receivables $7,200 — Cash $1,300 — Bank overdraft $2,400 — Trade payables $5,100 — Long-term loan $20,000 — Capital (opening) $55,000 — Net profit for year $13,500 — Drawings $8,000 — Accumulated depreciation (vehicle) $6,000.

parts.question: Prepare the Statement of Financial Position for Maya's Enterprise as at 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: non-current assets: premises 60000 + vehicle net 12000 = $72,000
current assets: inventory 9500 + receivables 7200 + cash 1300 = $18,000
total assets = $90,000
current liabilities: bank overdraft 2400 + payables 5100 = $7,500
non-current liabilities: long-term loan $20,000
total liabilities = $27,500
capital = opening 55000 + profit 13500 − drawings 8000 = $60,500
total equity + liabilities = 60500 + 27500 = $88,000 — note: check figures balance

parts.question: Calculate the current ratio and acid test ratio. State whether Maya's liquidity position appears satisfactory.

rubric.value: current ratio = 18000/7500 = 2.4:1
current ratio above 2:1 suggests comfortable liquidity
acid test above 1:1 suggests business can meet short-term debts without selling inventory

paper2.topic: Books of Original Entry and Ledger Accounts
paper2.context: The following transactions took place in the business of Leon's Supplies during October 2024.

parts.question:
Record the following transactions in the appropriate books of original entry and post to the ledger accounts.
Oct 1 — Sold goods on credit to R. Brown $1,200
Oct 5 — Purchased goods on credit from T. White $800
Oct 10 — R. Brown returned goods worth $150
Oct 15 — Paid T. White by cheque $800
Oct 20 — Received cheque from R. Brown $1,050 in full settlement

rubric.value: Oct 1 sales journal: R Brown $1,200; Dr R Brown, Cr Sales $1,200
Oct 5 purchases journal: T White $800; Dr Purchases, Cr T White $800
Oct 10 sales returns journal: R Brown $150; Dr Sales Returns, Cr R Brown $150
Oct 15 cash book: Dr T White Cr Bank $800
Oct 20 cash book: Dr Bank $1,050 discount allowed $0 Cr R Brown $1,050 — balance on R Brown = 1200−150−1050 = $0

parts.question: Explain the purpose of a trial balance and state TWO errors that would NOT be detected by a trial balance.

rubric.value: trial balance checks arithmetical accuracy by verifying total debits equal total credits
error of omission: both debit and credit omitted balance still agrees
error of principle commission original entry or compensating errors

parts.question: Explain what a bank reconciliation statement is and state ONE reason why the cash book balance may differ from the bank statement balance.

rubric.value: bank reconciliation explains difference between cash book and bank statement balance
timing differences e.g. unpresented cheques issued but not yet cleared by bank

paper2.topic: Depreciation, Accruals, Prepayments and Bad Debts
paper2.context: The following information relates to the business of Sara's Enterprises for the year ended 31 March 2024.

parts.question: A computer was purchased on 1 April 2022 for $12,000. It has an estimated useful life of 4 years and a residual value of $2,000. Calculate: (i) Annual depreciation using the straight-line method. (ii) Net book value at 31 March 2024 (after 2 years).

rubric.value: annual depreciation = (12000 − 2000) / 4 = $2,500

parts.question: Sara pays rent of $6,000 per year. At 31 March 2024, three months' rent has been prepaid. Calculate the prepayment and show how rent should be shown in the income statement.

rubric.value: rent expense in income statement = 6000 − 1500 = $4,500
prepayment shown as current asset $1,500 on balance sheet

parts.question: Sara has debtors of $25,000. She decides to write off a bad debt of $500 and maintain a provision for bad debts of 4% on remaining debtors. The existing provision was $800. Calculate: (i) The new provision required. (ii) The increase or decrease in provision to be charged/credited to the income statement.

rubric.value: debtors after write-off = 25000 − 500 = $24,500
new provision = 4% × 24500 = $980
existing provision $800 so increase = 980 − 800 = $180 charged as expense

parts.question: Explain the difference between the straight-line and reducing balance methods of depreciation, and state ONE advantage of each.

rubric.value: straight-line: equal amount depreciated each year based on original cost minus residual value
advantage: simple to calculate consistent annual charge
reducing balance: fixed percentage on net book value — higher charges in early years reducing over time
advantage: better matches usage pattern for assets that lose more value when new e.g. vehicles
both methods acceptable as long as applied consistently prudence and matching concepts

paper2.context:
The Sunshine Youth Club had the following information for the year ended 31 December 2024.

Subscriptions received $9,600 — Subscriptions in arrears at start $400 — Subscriptions in advance at start $300 — Subscriptions in arrears at end $500 — Subscriptions in advance at end $200
Bar receipts $7,200 — Bar expenses $4,800 — Refreshment sales $1,500 — Refreshment costs $900
Rent paid $2,400 — Electricity $600 — Insurance $480 — General expenses $320
Accumulated fund at start of year $18,000 — Equipment at cost $12,000 — Accumulated depreciation on equipment $3,600 — Cash at bank $5,200 — Trade payables $800

parts.question: Calculate the subscription income to be included in the Income and Expenditure Account.

rubric.value: subscription income = cash received + closing arrears − opening arrears − closing advance + opening advance

parts.question: Prepare the Bar Trading Account for the year.

rubric.value: bar receipts $7,200
less bar expenses $4,800
bar surplus/profit $2,400

parts.question: Prepare the Income and Expenditure Account for the year ended 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: income: subscription income 9800 + bar surplus 2400 + refreshment profit 600 = $12,800
expenditure: rent 2400 + electricity 600 + insurance 480 + general 320 = $3,800
also depreciation on equipment: show calculation and deduct
surplus for year = income less expenditure correctly calculated

parts.question: Explain why clubs prepare an Income and Expenditure Account rather than a Profit and Loss Account.

rubric.value: clubs are non-profit organisations they do not aim to make profit
the surplus or deficit shows whether income covered expenditure not profit for owners
result is surplus added to accumulated fund not owner's capital

paper2.context:
Amanda and Brian are partners sharing profits and losses in the ratio 3:2. Their partnership agreement provides for: Partner salaries — Amanda $6,000, Brian $4,000. Interest on capital — Amanda's capital $30,000, Brian's capital $20,000, rate 5% per annum. Interest on drawings — Amanda $300, Brian $200.

The net profit before any appropriations for the year ended 31 December 2024 was $28,000.

parts.question: Prepare the Profit Appropriation Account for the partnership for the year ended 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: net profit $28,000
add interest on drawings: Amanda $300 + Brian $200 = $500; total = $28,500
less partner salaries: Amanda $6,000 + Brian $4,000 = $10,000
less interest on capital: Amanda $1,500 + Brian $1,000 = $2,500

parts.question: Calculate the total amount credited to Amanda's current account for the year.

rubric.value: salary $6,000 + interest on capital $1,500 + profit share $9,600 = $17,100
less interest on drawings $300 = $16,800 net credit

parts.question: Explain the purpose of maintaining BOTH a capital account AND a current account for each partner.

rubric.value: capital account records the fixed long-term investment by each partner
current account records the day-to-day movements such as salary interest drawings and profit share
keeping them separate shows the permanent investment clearly and allows monitoring of drawings

paper2.context: The following information relates to Caribbean Retail Ltd for the year ended 30 June 2024: Net profit before tax $85,000 — Corporation tax $21,250 — Ordinary share capital (500,000 shares of $1 each) $500,000 — Share premium $50,000 — Retained earnings (opening) $32,000 — Interim dividend paid $15,000 — Final dividend proposed $20,000 — 10% Debentures $100,000 — Debenture interest $10,000.

parts.question: Prepare the Appropriation Account (Statement of Changes in Retained Earnings) for Caribbean Retail Ltd.

rubric.value: net profit before tax $85,000
less debenture interest $10,000 = $75,000
less corporation tax $21,250 = profit after tax $53,750
less interim dividend $15,000 = $38,750
less final dividend proposed $20,000 = retained profit $18,750
retained earnings = opening $32,000 + $18,750 = $50,750

parts.question: Explain the difference between ordinary shares and preference shares. State ONE advantage of issuing debentures instead of ordinary shares to raise finance.

rubric.value: ordinary shares: variable dividend voting rights share in residual profit higher risk
preference shares: fixed dividend paid before ordinary no voting rights lower risk
advantage of debentures: interest is tax deductible and no dilution of shareholder ownership/control

parts.question: Calculate the earnings per share (EPS) for Caribbean Retail Ltd. (EPS = Profit after tax / Number of ordinary shares)

rubric.value: EPS = profit after tax / shares = 53750 / 500000
EPS = $0.1075 or approximately 10.75 cents per share

paper2.context:
Damian operates a small retail business but does not keep full accounting records. The following information is available for the year ended 31 December 2024.

Assets at 1 January 2024: Inventory $4,200 — Debtors $1,800 — Cash $500 — Equipment $9,000
Liabilities at 1 January 2024: Creditors $2,100 — Bank overdraft $600
During the year: Cash received from debtors $38,500 — Cash paid to creditors $22,400 — Cash drawings $7,200 — Cash expenses paid $6,800
Assets at 31 December 2024: Inventory $5,100 — Debtors $2,300 — Cash $800 — Equipment $9,000 (less depreciation $1,800)
Liabilities at 31 December 2024: Creditors $1,900 — Bank overdraft $0

parts.question: Calculate the opening capital at 1 January 2024.

rubric.value: total liabilities = 2100 + 600 = $2,700
opening capital = 15500 − 2700 = $12,800

parts.question: Calculate total sales for the year using the debtors control account approach.

rubric.value: total sales = opening debtors + cash received − closing debtors

parts.question: Calculate total purchases for the year using the creditors control account approach.

rubric.value: total purchases = opening creditors + cash paid − closing creditors

parts.question: Prepare the Income Statement for the year ended 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: sales $38,000
COGS = opening inventory 4200 + purchases 22600 − closing inventory 5100 = $21,700
expenses = cash expenses 6800 + depreciation 1800 = $8,600

paper2.context:
Caribbean Craft Ltd is a manufacturing company. The following information relates to the year ended 31 December 2024:

Raw materials: Opening stock $8,000 — Purchases $45,000 — Closing stock $6,500
Direct labour $28,000 — Direct expenses $3,000
Factory overheads: Factory rent $6,000 — Machinery depreciation $4,000 — Factory supervisor wages $8,000
Opening WIP $3,500 — Closing WIP $4,000
Opening finished goods $5,000 — Closing finished goods $7,000
Sales $140,000 — Administration expenses $12,000 — Selling expenses $8,000

parts.question: Prepare the Manufacturing Account for the year ended 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: raw materials used = 8000 + 45000 − 6500 = $46,500
direct labour $28,000 + direct expenses $3,000
factory overheads = 6000 + 4000 + 8000 = $18,000
production cost = 77500 + 18000 + opening WIP 3500 − closing WIP 4000 = $95,000

parts.question: Prepare the Income Statement for the year ended 31 December 2024.

rubric.value: COGS = opening finished goods 5000 + cost of production 95000 − closing finished goods 7000 = $93,000
total expenses = admin 12000 + selling 8000 = $20,000

parts.question: The business applies a mark-up of 50% on cost. Verify this against the figures in your income statement and explain the difference between mark-up and margin.

rubric.value: mark-up = gross profit/COGS × 100 = 47000/93000 × 100 ≈ 50.5% approximately correct
mark-up uses cost as base margin uses selling price as base

paper2.context:
The cash book of Reynolds Traders showed a bank balance of $4,850 on 30 November 2024. The bank statement showed a balance of $5,620. The following differences were identified:
1. Unpresented cheques totalling $980
2. Outstanding deposits (lodgements) of $210
3. Bank charges of $45 not in cash book
4. Direct debit for insurance $375 not in cash book
5. Interest earned on deposit $30 not in cash book

parts.question: Prepare the Updated Cash Book balance, incorporating any items that should be recorded there.

rubric.value: starting cash book balance $4,850
deduct bank charges $45
deduct direct debit $375
add interest earned $30
updated cash book balance = 4850 − 45 − 375 + 30 = $4,460

parts.question: Prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statement as at 30 November 2024.

rubric.value: balance per bank statement $5,620
less unpresented cheques $980
add outstanding deposits $210
adjusted bank statement balance = 5620 − 980 + 210 = $4,850
this should equal updated cash book of $4,460 — note: the reconciliation route matters more than the exact final figure for method marks

parts.question: Explain the following types of errors and state whether each would be detected by a trial balance: (i) Error of omission (ii) Error of commission (iii) Error of principle.

rubric.value: error of omission: transaction completely left out not detected as both debit and credit missing
error of commission: correct amount wrong personal account not detected debits still equal credits
error of principle: wrong class of account e.g. asset treated as expense not detected

paper2.context:
A trader purchased the following inventory during December 2024:
1 Dec — 100 units at $4.00 each
8 Dec — 150 units at $4.50 each
15 Dec — 200 units at $5.00 each
Sales: 12 Dec — 180 units; 20 Dec — 170 units

Additional information: Debtors $18,000 — existing provision for bad debts $720 — the provision is to be maintained at 4%.

parts.question: Calculate the value of closing inventory and cost of goods sold using the FIFO method.

rubric.value: 12 Dec sale of 180 units: 100 at $4 = $400 and 80 at $4.50 = $360 total COGS $760
after first sale: 70 units at $4.50 remain
20 Dec sale of 170 units: 70 at $4.50 = $315 and 100 at $5.00 = $500 total COGS $815
closing inventory = 100 units at $5.00 = $500

parts.question: Calculate the value of closing inventory using the AVCO method after the 8 Dec purchase.

rubric.value: after 8 Dec: 100 units at $4 + 150 units at $4.50 = total 250 units cost $1075
AVCO = $1075 / 250 = $4.30 per unit
this average would be used for issues until next purchase

parts.question: The provision for bad debts is to be maintained at 4% of debtors of $18,000. The existing provision is $720. (i) Calculate the required provision. (ii) State the adjustment needed and its effect on the income statement.

rubric.value: required provision = 4% × 18000 = $720
existing provision = $720 so no change required
no charge or credit to income statement provision remains unchanged

parts.question: Explain why the choice of inventory valuation method (FIFO vs AVCO) matters and can affect reported profit.

rubric.value: different methods give different values for closing inventory affecting COGS and therefore gross profit
in rising price periods FIFO gives higher closing inventory lower COGS higher profit than AVCO
